title: Punk Rock Chicks? These Baby Birds Just Won a Major Photo Contest
summary:
They squabble. They peck. They have spiky feathers that look like tiny mohawks. Three baby egrets have just been named the grand-prize winners of Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photography contest, and they're already being called "The Punk Rockers." Photographer Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian from Perkiomenville, Pennsylvania, took the winning shot at a rookery (a breeding ground for birds) in Ocean City, New Jersey. "Their little necks and heads were writhing," she said. "One would peck. The other would peck back." The chicks, each no larger than a golf ball when curled up, were playing and fighting under a ramp. Their parent looms in the background, with long legs and imposing feet that make the fluffy chicks look even smaller. "The scale jump between the long legs and gnarly, imposing feet compared to the fluffy heads below is remarkable," said Smithsonian creative director Maria Keehan.

The contest drew more than 17,000 submissions from 108 countries. Six other winners were also announced, each capturing a unique moment. One photo shows a barefoot devotee during the Hindu festival of Shivaratri, lighting oil lamps in an act of prayer. Another captures a 14-year-old Navajo girl named DelShay Henio during her Kinaaldá ceremony, a four-day ritual welcoming her to womanhood. She incorporated her horse Hazel into the ceremony. "It's a quiet, soft portrait of a young woman looking toward her future," said photographer Cara Carpenter. A conservation photographer from Johannesburg, Jenna West, won for her image of a chameleon in a garden. "I just thought it was really, really beautiful," she said. Other winners include a dramatic Holy Week procession in Sicily, a Mundari child cleaning a bull's horns in South Sudan, and an aerial view of the circular Oppo Chang An R&D Center in China. Burke, who traded her bird-watching binoculars for a camera in 2014, hopes her photo reminds people to protect wildlife. "We need to save species like this and save moments like this," she said.
why_it_matters: This story shows how photography can highlight the beauty of wildlife and raise awareness about conservation, but it also raises questions about whether winning a contest translates into real action to protect endangered species.

keywords.explanation: a type of heron bird, often white
a spiky hairstyle like a punk rocker
a small ball used in the sport of golf

questions.question: How many submissions did the Smithsonian contest receive?
options: over 17,000 from 108 countries
over 10,000 from 50 countries
15,000 from 100 countries
20,000 from 120 countries

questions.question: Why did the photographer Jacqueline Burke start taking pictures?
options: She always wanted to be a photographer
She traded her bird-watching binoculars for a camera in 2014
She won a camera in a contest
She was given a camera by the Smithsonian
questions.correct_answer: She traded her bird-watching binoculars for a camera in 2014

options: It is the best photo she has ever seen
The scale jump between long legs and fluffy heads is remarkable
The chicks are no larger than a golf ball
The parent bird is imposing
questions.correct_answer: The scale jump between long legs and fluffy heads is remarkable

the 14-year-old Navajo girl on a horse
the chameleon in the garden
the Holy Week procession in Sicily

options: It was an endangered species
It was really, really beautiful

options: That it would win more prizes
That it would remind people to protect wildlife
That it would be published in a book
That her family would be proud
questions.correct_answer: That it would remind people to protect wildlife

background_read: The Smithsonian Magazine Photography Contest has been held annually for 23 years, attracting photographers worldwide.
Egrets are wading birds belonging to the heron family; they are known for their elegant white plumage and are protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act in the US.
The rookery in Ocean City, New Jersey, is a known breeding site for egrets and other waterbirds along the Atlantic coast.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Three baby egrets with mohawk-like feathers win top prize
KEY EVENT: Photographer Jacqueline Burke captures squabbling chicks at a rookery
├─ WHERE: Ocean City, New Jersey rookery
└─ SIZE: Chicks no larger than golf balls
CONTEXT: Smithsonian contest statistics (17,000+ entries, 108 countries)
├─ Navajo girl DelShay Henio during Kinaaldá ceremony
└─ Other: Shivaratri festival, Holy Week, Mundari child, Chinese building
PHOTOGRAPHER'S MESSAGE: Need to protect wildlife and moments like these

perspectives.description: The photo draws attention to egrets and their habitats, encouraging people to appreciate and protect birds. It proves that even common species can be fascinating.
While the photo is charming, winning a contest does little to stop habitat loss or address the real threats birds face, such as pollution and climate change. It risks being just a feel-good moment.

This is a classic example of 'cute animal story' journalism—designed to engage readers emotionally. Such stories often go viral but seldom lead to sustained engagement with serious conservation issues.

The photographer hopes her image will inspire people to protect species. With proper use, the publicity could support local conservation efforts, but that depends on follow-up action by media and organizations.
